# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** mark split: **Part A = 2 marks (definition/short)**, **Part B = 14 marks** with internal choice and two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

## Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Frequency Response. Mention the two graphical plots used to represent it.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Frequency Response** is the *steady-state response of a stable LTI system to a sinusoidal input*, observed as the input frequency $\omega$ is varied while its amplitude is held constant. It is represented by two graphs:
>
> 1. **Magnitude plot** — $\vert G(j\omega)\vert$ (in dB) versus $\log_{10}\omega$
> 2. **Phase plot** — $\angle G(j\omega)$ (in degrees) versus $\log_{10}\omega$
>
> Together they form the **Bode plot**.

### **Q2. State the significance of Gain Margin and Phase Margin in control system design.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

> - **Gain Margin ($K_g$):** It is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be *increased* before the closed-loop system reaches the *instability threshold* (where phase = $-180°$ at unity magnitude). A large $K_g$ implies the system can tolerate large gain variations without losing stability.
>
> - **Phase Margin ($\gamma$):** It is the additional phase lag, measured at the *gain crossover frequency* $\omega_{gc}$, that the system can withstand before becoming unstable. A larger $\gamma$ implies *more damping* and *less overshoot*.
>
> **Typical design target (KTU standard):** $\gamma \ge 45°$ and $K_g \ge 6$ dB for a well-damped stable response.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**For the open-loop transfer function**

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Bode magnitude and phase plots using asymptotic approximations. Determine the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ and **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **gain margin** and **phase margin**, and comment on the **closed-loop stability** of the corresponding unity-feedback system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Write the frequency response function:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)} = \frac{10}{j\omega \cdot \sqrt{\omega^2+4}\cdot\sqrt{\omega^2+25}}\;\angle\;\theta(\omega)$$

where

$$\theta(\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/5)$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude in dB:**

$$A(\omega) = 20\log 10 - 20\log\omega - 20\log\sqrt{\omega^2+4} - 20\log\sqrt{\omega^2+25}$$

**Step 3 — Corner frequencies:**
- Pole at origin → contributes $-20$ dB/decade from $\omega = 0$ onwards.
- Corner at $\omega_1 = 2$ rad/s → slope changes by $-20$ dB/dec.
- Corner at $\omega_2 = 5$ rad/s → slope changes by another $-20$ dB/dec.

**Step 4 — Asymptote segments:**

| Frequency Range | Slope | Equation |
|-----------------|:-----:|----------|
| $\omega < 2$ | $-20$ dB/dec | $A = 20\log 10 - 20\log\omega$ |
| $2 \le \omega < 5$ | $-40$ dB/dec | $A = A(2) - 40\log(\omega/2)$ |
| $\omega \ge 5$ | $-60$ dB/dec | $A = A(5) - 60\log(\omega/5)$ |

**Step 5 — Compute gain crossover (where $A=0$):**

In the range $2 \le \omega < 5$, $A(\omega) = 14 - 40\log(\omega/2)$ where $A(2) = 20 - 20\log 2 \approx 14$ dB.

Setting $A = 0$: $40\log(\omega/2) = 14 \Rightarrow \log(\omega/2) = 0.35 \Rightarrow \omega/2 = 2.24$

$$\boxed{\omega_{gc} \approx 4.47\text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:**

$$\theta(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(4.47/2) - \tan^{-1}(4.47/5)$$

$$= -90° - 65.9° - 41.8° = -197.7°$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Phase crossover frequency** (where phase = $-180°$):

As computed in §3.4 above:

$$\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16\text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{14}\cdot\sqrt{35}} = \frac{1}{7}$$

$$A_{pc} = 20\log(1/7) = -16.9\text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Gain Margin:**

$$K_g = -A_{pc} = +16.9\text{ dB} \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_g(\text{linear}) = 7$$

**Step 4 — Phase Margin:**

$$\gamma = 180° + \theta(\omega_{gc}) = 180° - 197.7° = -17.7°$$

**Step 5 — Stability Comment:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Phase margin is negative ($-17.7°$); although $K_g > 0$ dB, the negative phase margin means the closed-loop unity-feedback system is UNSTABLE in practice. The Bode stability criterion requires *both* margins to be positive for guaranteed stability.**

**Conclusion:** The system is **unstable** in closed-loop unity feedback. A lead compensator is required to add positive phase at $\omega_{gc}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**A second-order unity-feedback system has the open-loop transfer function**

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)}$$

**(a)** Determine the value of $K$ for which the **damping ratio** $\zeta = 0.5$. Hence compute the **resonant peak** $M_r$ and the **resonant frequency** $\omega_r$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same $K$, calculate the **bandwidth** $\omega_b$ and the **phase margin** $\gamma$ at the gain crossover frequency. Discuss how $\gamma$ relates to $\zeta$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Step 1 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s^2 + 4s + K = 0$$

Standard form: $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$

**Step 2 — Equate coefficients:**

$$2\zeta\omega_n = 4 \quad,\quad \omega_n^2 = K$$

Given $\zeta = 0.5$:

$$2(0.5)\omega_n = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_n = 4\text{ rad/s}$$

$$K = \omega_n^2 = 16$$

**Step 3 — Resonant frequency (valid because $\zeta < 0.707$):**

$$\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = 4\sqrt{1 - 2(0.25)} = 4\sqrt{0.5} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83\text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Resonant peak:**

$$M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2(0.5)\sqrt{1-0.25}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.75}} = \frac{1}{0.866} \approx 1.155$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Bandwidth:**

$$\omega_b = \omega_n\sqrt{(1-2\zeta^2) + \sqrt{4\zeta^4 - 4\zeta^2 + 2}}$$

$$= 4\sqrt{0.5 + \sqrt{4(0.0625) - 4(0.25) + 2}}$$

$$= 4\sqrt{0.5 + \sqrt{0.25 - 1 + 2}} = 4\sqrt{0.5 + \sqrt{1.25}}$$

$$= 4\sqrt{0.5 + 1.118} = 4\sqrt{1.618} \approx 5.09\text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Gain crossover frequency:**

From $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+4)} = \dfrac{16}{j\omega(j\omega+4)}$

$$\vert G(j\omega)\vert = \frac{16}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+16}} = 1$$

Solving: $256 = \omega^2(\omega^2+16) = \omega^4 + 16\omega^2$. Let $x = \omega^2$:

$$x^2 + 16x - 256 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x = \frac{-16 + \sqrt{256 + 1024}}{2} = \frac{-16 + \sqrt{1280}}{2} \approx 9.89$$

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 3.145\text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:**

$$\angle G = -90° - \tan^{-1}(3.145/4) = -90° - 38.2° = -128.2°$$

**Step 4 — Phase margin:**

$$\gamma = 180° - 128.2° = 51.8°$$

**Step 5 — Discussion:**

> Using the empirical relation $\zeta \approx \gamma/100$ gives $\zeta \approx 0.518$, which is close to the true value $0.5$. The slight discrepancy arises because the empirical formula is approximate; the *exact* relation between $\gamma$ and $\zeta$ for a 2nd-order system is
>
> $$\gamma = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\frac{2\zeta}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1+4\zeta^4}-2\zeta^2}}\right]$$
>
> confirming that **higher damping ratio ⇒ larger phase margin**, and that phase margin is an excellent surrogate for closed-loop damping.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 2 in `20 log`!** Using $10\log$ instead of $20\log$ for magnitude in dB costs full 1 mark.
> 2. **Phase-crossover confusion.** Many students evaluate magnitude at the *gain-crossover* frequency and call it gain margin — that is incorrect. $K_g$ uses $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180°$), *not* $\omega_{gc}$.
> 3. **Resonance condition reversed.** Writing $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{2\zeta^2 - 1}$ (negative sign lost) — the correct expression is $\omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$.
> 4. **Slope sign error on Bode plot.** Adding $+20$ dB/decade at a pole rather than $-20$ dB/decade — poles *reduce* magnitude.
> 5. **Bandwidth for overdamped systems.** Using the bandwidth formula when $\zeta \ge 0.707$ — the closed-loop system has no peak but the formula is still valid for the $-3$ dB point.
> 6. **Mixing open-loop and closed-loop Bode plots.** Frequency response specifications ($M_r,\omega_r,\omega_b$) refer to the *closed-loop* transfer function, while gain/phase margin refer to the *open-loop* transfer function.
> 7. **Stability inference from Bode plot.** A *negative* phase margin with a *positive* gain margin still means the system is **unstable** — both margins must be positive.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Keep this on your formula sheet side margin!**

- **Frequency Response:** Steady-state output to sinusoidal input; obtained by $s = j\omega$ substitution.
- **Magnitude in dB:** $A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega)\vert$ (always use 20, not 10).
- **Resonant Frequency:** $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ — *only for $0 \le \zeta < 0.707$.*
- **Resonant Peak:** $M_r = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ — same condition.
- **Bandwidth:** $\omega_b = \omega_n\sqrt{(1-2\zeta^2)+\sqrt{4\zeta^4-4\zeta^2+2}}$.
- **Bode plot axes:** Magnitude in dB vs $\log\omega$; Phase in degrees vs $\log\omega$.
- **Corner frequency:** $\omega_c = 1/T$ for term $(1+j\omega T)$ in denominator.
- **Slope change at corner:** $\pm 20$ dB/dec per simple pole/zero; $\pm 40$ dB/dec per complex pair.
- **Gain Crossover $\omega_{gc}$:** Frequency where magnitude curve = 0 dB.
- **Phase Crossover $\omega_{pc}$:** Frequency where phase curve = $-180°$.
- **Phase Margin $\gamma$:** $\gamma = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$.
- **Gain Margin $K_g$:** $K_g = 1\,\big/\,\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert$ (linear) or $-20\log\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert$ dB.
- **Stability criterion (Bode):** Stable iff $\gamma > 0°$ *and* $K_g > 0$ dB.
- **Design rule of thumb:** Aim for $\gamma \ge 45°$ and $K_g \ge 6$ dB.
- **PM ↔ $\zeta$ approximation:** $\zeta \approx \gamma/100$ (empirical but exam-useful).
- **$M_r$ ↔ PM approximation:** $M_r \approx 1/\sin\gamma$ for small $\zeta$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routh-Hurwitz Criterion, Root Locus Techniques, Concepts of Relative Stability. Overview of Compensators for Design Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT413 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routh-Hurwitz Criterion, Root Locus Techniques, Concepts of Relative Stability. Overview of Compensators for Design Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion

### Formal Definition
The **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** is an algebraic, purely analytical method (introduced by Edward John Routh in 1877 and independently reformulated by Adolf Hurwitz) used to determine the **absolute stability** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time system. It provides a necessary and sufficient condition about the location of the closed-loop poles in the left-half of the complex s-plane by examining the coefficients of the characteristic equation, without actually computing the roots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For a characteristic polynomial $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$, the Routh-Hurwitz test determines (a) the number of roots in the right-half s-plane and (b) the critical (marginal) value of the gain $K$ beyond which the system becomes unstable.

The characteristic equation of a standard $n^{th}$-order closed-loop system has the canonical form:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

A *necessary* condition for stability is that all coefficients $a_i$ must be **real, positive, and non-zero** (no missing terms and no negative signs). The *sufficient* condition is verified by constructing the **Routh Array**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are the captain of a ship steering toward a harbor. The ship's path is determined by equations (characteristic equation). You don't need to plot the entire trajectory to know whether you'll reach the harbor safely. By merely checking the "signs" and "balance" of the path coefficients (Routh array), you can predict whether the journey will end in safe waters (stable) or crash on a rock (unstable). The Routh array is essentially a "numerical report card" that tells you about hidden dangers without solving the polynomial.

---

## 1.2 Root Locus Techniques

### Formal Definition
The **Root Locus** is a graphical technique developed by Walter R. Evans (1948) in which the trajectories (loci) of the closed-loop poles of a feedback system are plotted in the complex s-plane as a single parameter — typically the open-loop gain $K$ — is varied from $0$ to $+\infty$. Each point on the locus represents a possible closed-loop pole location for a specific value of $K$, and the locus is therefore a **map of system stability behavior** as gain changes.

> [!NOTE]
> The root locus is the locus of points in the s-plane where the angle of the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ is an odd multiple of $180^\circ$, and the magnitude condition $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1/K$ determines the value of $K$ at any point on the locus.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a lake (the s-plane) with several fixed lighthouses (open-loop poles, marked with $\times$) and buoys (open-loop zeros, marked with $\bigcirc$). As a search-light beam (the root locus) sweeps the lake, its path is determined by the angle condition: it bends to keep the angles of arrival from poles and zeros balanced in a specific way. The light always **starts** at each open-loop pole when $K=0$ and **ends** at a zero (or at infinity) when $K \to \infty$. Wherever the beam crosses the imaginary axis (lake shore), the system is at the threshold of instability.

---

## 1.3 Concepts of Relative Stability

### Formal Definition
While absolute stability only asks the question *"Is the system stable?"*, **relative stability** asks *"How stable is the system?"* — that is, how *robust* the closed-loop poles are to disturbances, parameter variations, and how fast the transient response will decay. It is quantified in the time-domain by the **damping ratio ($\zeta$)** and **settling time ($t_s$)**, and in the frequency-domain by the classical metrics: **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gain Margin (GM)** is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be multiplied at the **phase crossover frequency ($\omega_{pc}$)** before the system becomes unstable.
> **Phase Margin (PM)** is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be tolerated at the **gain crossover frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)** before instability sets in.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a tightrope walker (the closed-loop system). Absolute stability asks: *"Will the walker fall off the rope?"* Relative stability asks: *"How far is the walker from the edge of the rope?"* The **gain margin** is the extra wind gust (gain increase) the walker can survive, and the **phase margin** is the extra wobble (phase delay) the walker can absorb before losing balance.

---

## 1.4 Overview of Compensators for Design Improvement

### Formal Definition
A **compensator** (or *compensating network*) is an additional transfer function $G_c(s)$ inserted in cascade with the plant $G(s)$ (or in a feedback path) to alter the open-loop frequency response so that the closed-loop system meets prescribed **steady-state** and **transient** specifications — specifically, raising the gain for better steady-state accuracy without sacrificing the phase margin (transient stability).

The three principal compensator types are:

| Type | Transfer Function Form | Primary Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead Compensator** | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \alpha \tau s}, \;\; 0 < \alpha < 1$ | Improves transient response / stability margin |
| **Lag Compensator** | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \beta \tau s}, \;\; \beta > 1$ | Improves steady-state error (raises low-frequency gain) |
| **Lead-Lag Compensator** | $G_c(s) = \left(\dfrac{1 + \tau_1 s}{1 + \alpha \tau_1 s}\right) \left(\dfrac{1 + \tau_2 s}{1 + \beta \tau_2 s}\right)$ | Improves both transient and steady-state response |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 expects students to be able to identify the **type** of compensator, write its transfer function in **pole-zero form**, sketch its **Bode plot**, and qualitatively describe its impact on system performance — full analytical compensator design using bode plots is usually covered in Module 5.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine driving a sluggish car (the uncompensated plant). A **lead compensator** is like installing a **turbocharger** — it gives a quick burst of response (improves damping/PM). A **lag compensator** is like adding **more fuel at low throttle** — it gives better cruising efficiency (reduces steady-state error). A **lead-lag compensator** combines both for an all-round upgrade.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Routh-Hurwitz Criterion — Operational Logic

### Step 1: Form the Characteristic Equation
Start from the closed-loop transfer function denominator: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, and clear fractions to obtain:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

### Step 2: Necessary Condition
All coefficients must be present, **real**, and **same sign (positive)**. If any coefficient is missing, negative, or zero, the system *may* be unstable — but the Routh array is needed to confirm.

### Step 3: Construct the Routh Array

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
s^n & a_0 & a_2 & a_4 & a_6 \\
s^{n-1} & a_1 & a_3 & a_5 & a_7 \\
s^{n-2} & b_1 & b_2 & b_3 & 0 \\
s^{n-3} & c_1 & c_2 & c_3 & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
s^0 & a_n & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

where the entries are computed as:

$$b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \quad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}, \quad b_3 = \frac{a_1 a_6 - a_0 a_7}{a_1}$$

$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1}, \quad c_2 = \frac{b_1 a_5 - a_1 b_3}{b_1}, \quad \ldots$$

Each new row is constructed from the **two rows immediately above** it.

### Step 4: Apply the Stability Theorem
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Routh-Hurwitz Stability Theorem:** The number of roots of the characteristic equation in the right-half s-plane equals the **number of sign changes** in the **first column** of the Routh array. For absolute stability, **no sign change** must occur in the first column.

### Special Case 1: Zero in the First Column (but the row is not all zeros)
Replace the zero by a small positive number $\varepsilon$ and continue construction, then take the limit $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.

### Special Case 2: Entire Row of Zeros
This indicates **symmetrically placed roots** about the origin (e.g., $\pm j\omega$, $\pm \sigma$, pairs of real roots with opposite signs). Form the **Auxiliary Equation** $A(s)$ from the row directly above the zero row (using even powers of $s$). Then:
- Differentiate $A(s)$ with respect to $s$ to get $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$.
- Replace the zero row with the coefficients of $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$.
- The roots of $A(s) = 0$ are the **critical frequencies** where the locus crosses the imaginary axis.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: A row of zeros does **not** mean the system is stable. It means marginal stability with roots on the $j\omega$ axis — and the auxiliary equation gives the *imaginary axis crossing points*.

---

## 2.2 Root Locus — Construction Rules

Let the open-loop transfer function be:

$$KG(s)H(s) = \frac{K \prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{s^N \prod_{j=1}^{p-N}(s - p_j)} = \frac{K \prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{k=1}^{n}(s - p_k)}$$

where $n$ is the total number of open-loop poles and $m$ is the total number of open-loop zeros.

| Rule # | Rule | Description |
|:---:|---|---|
| **1** | Symmetry | The locus is symmetric about the real axis of the s-plane. |
| **2** | Starting & Ending Points ($K=0$ and $K=\infty$) | The locus *starts* at $K=0$ at each open-loop pole ($n$ branches) and *ends* at $K=\infty$ at each open-loop zero ($m$ branches) or at infinity along asymptotes ($n-m$ branches go to infinity). |
| **3** | Real-Axis Segments | A point on the real axis is part of the locus only if the **total number of real poles and zeros to its right is odd**. |
| **4** | Number of Asymptotes | Number of asymptotes = $n - m$. |
| **5** | Angles of Asymptotes | $\theta_k = \dfrac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{n - m}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, (n-m-1)$ |
| **6** | Centroid (Intersection of Asymptotes) | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_k - \sum z_i}{n - m}$ |
| **7** | Breakaway / Break-in Points | Found by solving $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ on the real-axis segment of the locus, where $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(s)H(s)}$. Valid breakaway points are confirmed by the **angle of departure** test. |
| **8** | Angle of Departure from a Complex Pole $p_r$ | $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle(p_r - z_i) - \sum \angle(p_r - p_k), \quad k \ne r$ |
| **8** | Angle of Arrival at a Complex Zero $z_r$ | $\phi_a = 180^\circ - \sum \angle(z_r - z_i) + \sum \angle(z_r - p_k), \quad i \ne r$ |
| **9** | Intersection with $j\omega$-Axis | Found by applying Routh-Hurwitz to find the critical $K$ (and corresponding $s = j\omega$). |

> [!TIP]
> For a 3rd-order or higher system, the **angle of departure** from a complex pole is essential and frequently tested in KTU board problems. Memorize the formula and remember to subtract all pole angles and add all zero angles from the test point.

---

## 2.3 Relative Stability — Frequency-Domain Metrics

### Gain Margin
The gain margin is measured at the **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$, the frequency at which the open-loop phase angle is $-180^\circ$:

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \quad \text{(linear scale)}$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(GM) = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

> A **positive** GM (in dB) means the system is stable. The **higher** the GM, the greater the relative stability.

### Phase Margin
The phase margin is measured at the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$, the frequency at which the open-loop magnitude is unity (0 dB):

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) H(j\omega_{gc})$$

> A **positive** PM means the system is stable. Typical design targets: $PM = 30^\circ$ to $60^\circ$ for a well-damped response.

### Relationship to Damping Ratio
For a dominant second-order system:

$$\zeta \approx \frac{PM(\text{in degrees})}{100}, \quad \text{valid for } 0 \le PM \le 70^\circ$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Characteristic equation** | $1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$ | Foundation for all stability tests |
| **Routh array row element** | $b_j = \dfrac{a_{1} a_{2+j} - a_0 a_{3+j}}{a_1}$ | Building the Routh array |
| **Auxiliary equation** | $A(s) = $ polynomial from row above zero row | Finding $j\omega$ crossings |
| **Root locus angle condition** | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm (2k+1)180^\circ$ | Membership on the locus |
| **Root locus magnitude condition** | $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$ | Computing $K$ at a point |
| **Asymptote angle** | $\theta_k = \dfrac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{n-m}$ | Skeleton of the locus |
| **Centroid** | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_k - \sum z_i}{n-m}$ | Center of asymptote star |
| **Angle of departure** | $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle(p_r - z_i) - \sum \angle(p_r - p_k)$ | Direction near complex pole |
| **Breakaway equation** | $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0 \implies \dfrac{d}{ds}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{G(s)H(s)}\right] = 0$ | Real-axis break points |
| **Gain margin (dB)** | $GM_{dB} = -20 \log \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Relative stability in dB |
| **Phase margin** | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Relative stability in degrees |
| **Lead compensator** | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \alpha \tau s}, \;\; 0<\alpha<1$ | Improves transient/PM |
| **Lag compensator** | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \beta \tau s}, \;\; \beta>1$ | Improves steady-state error |
| **Lead-lag compensator** | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau_1 s}{1 + \alpha \tau_1 s} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \tau_2 s}{1 + \beta \tau_2 s}$ | Combines both effects |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace flight control systems** rely on Routh-Hurwitz as a pre-flight check to verify that an autopilot will not diverge at any operating gain.
- **Power system stabilizers** use root locus to find the optimal PSS gain that places generator-mode poles in a well-damped region.
- **DC motor speed control** loops are tuned using gain and phase margin specifications to ensure smooth speed response under load changes.
- **Compensators** are universally used in industrial servo drives, robotic arm controllers, and disk-drive read-head positioning systems to meet bandwidth and accuracy specs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Routh-Hurwitz Criterion

**Problem.** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion:
1. Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable.
2. Find the value of $K$ and the frequency $\omega$ at which the system becomes marginally stable.

### Step 1: Derive the Characteristic Equation
The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ (with $H(s)=1$):

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$

Multiply out:

$$s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expand $(s+2)(s+4)$:

$$(s+2)(s+4) = s^2 + 6s + 8$$

Multiply by $s$:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

So $a_0 = 1,\; a_1 = 6,\; a_2 = 8,\; a_3 = K$.

### Step 2: Verify the Necessary Condition
All coefficients must be positive: $1>0,\;6>0,\;8>0,\;K>0$. ✓ (so $K>0$ is a prerequisite for stability).

### Step 3: Construct the Routh Array

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{6 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot K}{6} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

Compute the $s^1$ row:

$$b_1 = \frac{(6)(8) - (1)(K)}{6} = \frac{48 - K}{6}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Stability Theorem
For stability, **every element in the first column must be positive**:

$$\text{First column} = \left[1,\;6,\;\frac{48-K}{6},\;K\right]$$

Conditions:
- $1 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $6 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 48 - K > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 48$
- $K > 0$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{0 < K < 48 \text{ for closed-loop stability}}$$

### Marginal Stability
At the boundary, $K = 48$. The auxiliary equation is the $s^2$ row:

$$A(s) = 6s^2 + K = 6s^2 + 48 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2 = -8 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j\sqrt{8} = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$$

Therefore at marginal stability:
- $K = 48$
- $\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$ rad/s

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Root Locus Construction

**Problem.** For the system $KG(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$, construct the root locus and find (a) asymptote angles and centroid, (b) breakaway point, (c) $j\omega$ crossing.

### Step 1: Identify Poles and Zeros
- Open-loop poles: $p_1 = 0,\; p_2 = -2,\; p_3 = -4$ (3 poles)
- Open-loop zeros: **none** ($m=0$)
- $n = 3,\; m = 0$

### Step 2: Asymptotes
Number of asymptotes: $n - m = 3 - 0 = 3$.

Angles:

$$\theta_k = \frac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{3}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=0: \quad \theta_0 &= \frac{180^\circ}{3} = 60^\circ \\
k=1: \quad \theta_1 &= \frac{3 \cdot 180^\circ}{3} = 180^\circ \\
k=2: \quad \theta_2 &= \frac{5 \cdot 180^\circ}{3} = 300^\circ = -60^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

Centroid:

$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum p_k - \sum z_i}{n-m} = \frac{(0 - 2 - 4) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

### Step 3: Real-Axis Segments (Rule 3)
Count real poles and zeros to the right of a trial point on the real axis:
- Between $0$ and $-2$: 3 real poles to the right → **odd → on locus** ✓
- Between $-2$ and $-4$: 2 real poles to the right → even → **NOT on locus** ✗
- Left of $-4$: 3 real poles to the right → **odd → on locus** ✓

So the real-axis segments are $[0, -2]$ and $(-\infty, -4]$.

### Step 4: Breakaway Point (Rule 7)
On the segment $[0,-2]$, two branches of the locus will break away. The breakaway point is found from:

$$K = -\frac{1}{G(s)H(s)} = -s(s+2)(s+4) = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s)$$

To minimize $K$ (since breakaway corresponds to $dK/ds = 0$):

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -\frac{d}{ds}\left[s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s\right] = -(3s^2 + 12s + 8) = 0$$

$$3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0$$

$$s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm 4\sqrt{3}}{6} = -2 \pm \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

Numerically: $s_1 = -0.845$ and $s_2 = -3.155$. Only $s = -0.845$ lies on the locus segment $[0,-2]$. ✓

**Breakaway point:** $\boxed{s = -0.845}$

### Step 5: Intersection with $j\omega$ Axis (Rule 9)
From the Routh-Hurwitz analysis in 3.1: $K = 48$, $s = \pm j\,2\sqrt{2}$.

### Step 6: Angle of Departure
There are no complex poles in this example, so the angle of departure formula is not needed.

### Step 7: Sketch Summary
- 3 branches start at $0, -2, -4$.
- Branches from $0$ and $-2$ move toward each other on $[0,-2]$ and break away at $-0.845$ into the complex plane.
- The third branch from $-4$ moves left along the real axis to $-\infty$ along the $180^\circ$ asymptote.
- All three branches go to infinity at angles $60^\circ, 180^\circ, 300^\circ$ from centroid $\sigma_A = -2$.
- The complex branches cross the $j\omega$ axis at $\omega = \pm 2\sqrt{2}$ when $K = 48$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Relative Stability (Gain & Phase Margin)

**Problem.** For the open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

find the gain margin and phase margin.

### Step 1: Find the Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$
The phase is:

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/5)$$

Set equal to $-180^\circ$:

$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}/5) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}/5) = 90^\circ$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1} A + \tan^{-1} B = 90^\circ$ when $AB = 1$:

$$\omega_{pc} \cdot \frac{\omega_{pc}}{5} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc}^2 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2: Compute Gain Margin
At $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{10}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2+1}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2+25}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5}\sqrt{6}\sqrt{30}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{900}} = \frac{10}{30} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$GM = \frac{1}{1/3} = 3 \quad \text{(linear)} \qquad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3) \approx 9.54 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 3: Find the Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$
Set magnitude equal to 1:

$$\frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+1}\sqrt{\omega^2+25}} = 1$$

$$\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+1}\sqrt{\omega^2+25} = 10$$

Square both sides:

$$\omega^2(\omega^2+1)(\omega^2+25) = 100$$

Let $x = \omega^2$:

$$x(x+1)(x+25) = 100 \;\Rightarrow\; x^3 + 26x^2 + 25x - 100 = 0$$

Solving numerically: $x \approx 2.36 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{gc} \approx 1.536$ rad/s.

### Step 4: Compute Phase Margin
At $\omega_{gc} = 1.536$:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(1.536) - \tan^{-1}(0.307)$$

$$= -90^\circ - 56.93^\circ - 17.08^\circ = -164.01^\circ$$

$$PM = 180^\circ + (-164.01^\circ) = 15.99^\circ \approx 16^\circ$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{GM \approx 9.54 \text{ dB}, \quad PM \approx 16^\circ}$$

The system is **stable** (both positive) but has a small phase margin → the transient response will be **highly oscillatory**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compensator Comparison

| Property | Lead | Lag | Lead-Lag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pole-zero pattern** | Zero to the *right* of pole on real axis | Pole to the *right* of zero (close to origin) | Lead part + lag part cascaded |
| **Phase contribution** | **Positive** (lead) — adds phase | **Negative** (lag) — small lag at $\omega_{gc}$ | Net positive phase boost + low-frequency gain |
| **Magnitude at low $\omega$** | 0 dB | $\approx -20\log(\beta)$ dB (attenuation) | Combines both |
| **Effect on $\omega_{gc}$** | Increases it → faster response | Decreases it (or keeps it) | Moderate change |
| **Effect on PM** | **Increases** PM | Slight decrease or no change | **Increases** PM |
| **Effect on $K_v$ (steady-state)** | No change (gain at $\omega=0$ is 1) | **Increases** $K_v$ by factor $\beta$ | **Increases** $K_v$ by factor $\beta$ |
| **Effect on transient** | Faster rise time, reduced overshoot | Minor effect | Faster rise + better steady-state |
| **Effect on bandwidth** | Increases | Decreases | Adjustable |
| **Primary trade-off** | Cannot improve $K_v$ | Cannot improve PM significantly | More components, more design effort |
| **Typical use** | Improving damping & speed | Reducing steady-state error | Meeting both speed & accuracy specs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **lead compensator** is essentially a high-pass filter that adds **positive phase** near $\omega_{gc}$, pulling the Nyquist contour away from the $-1$ point and increasing the phase margin. The **lag compensator** is a low-pass filter that boosts low-frequency magnitude, which raises $K_v$ and reduces steady-state error, with negligible effect on phase around $\omega_{gc}$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Routh Array Construction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Form Characteristic Equation<br>1 plus KG equals 0] --> B{All coefficients<br>real and positive?}
    B -- No --> C[System MAY be unstable<br>necessary condition violated]
    B -- Yes --> D[Construct Routh Array<br>row by row]
    D --> E{Any row of zeros?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Form Auxiliary Equation A of s<br>from row above<br>Differentiate to get dA over ds<br>Replace zero row]
    F --> G[Continue array construction]
    E -- No --> G
    G --> H{Any zero in<br>first column only?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Substitute epsilon<br>Take limit as eps approaches 0]
    H -- No --> J[Inspect first column]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Count sign changes in<br>first column]
    K --> L{Sign changes<br>equals zero?}
    L -- Yes --> M[System is Stable<br>All roots in LHP]
    L -- No --> N[System is Unstable<br>Number of sign changes equals<br>number of RHP roots]
```

## 4.2 Root Locus Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Loop Transfer Function<br>KG of s H of s] --> B[Identify poles and zeros<br>mark on s-plane]
    B --> C[Apply Rule 1: Symmetry<br>about real axis]
    C --> D[Apply Rule 2: n branches start at poles<br>m end at zeros<br>n minus m go to infinity]
    D --> E[Apply Rule 3: Real axis segments<br>odd count of poles zeros to the right]
    E --> F[Apply Rule 5 and 6:<br>Compute asymptote angles and centroid]
    F --> G[Apply Rule 7:<br>Solve dK over ds equals 0<br>Find breakaway points]
    G --> H[Apply Rule 8:<br>Compute angles of departure<br>and arrival at complex poles and zeros]
    H --> I[Apply Rule 9:<br>Use Routh Hurwitz to find<br>j omega axis crossing and critical K]
    I --> J[Sketch the complete root locus<br>Verify with K calculation<br>at selected points]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Compensator Placement in a Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R plus] --> Sum1[Summing Junction]
    Sum1 --> E[Error Signal E of s]
    E --> Gc[Gc of s<br>Compensator]
    Gc --> G[G of s<br>Plant]
    G --> Y[Y of s<br>Output]
    Y --> H[H of s<br>Sensor]
    H --> Sum1
    Y -.Feedback.-> Sum1
    style Gc fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Note:** The compensator $G_c(s)$ is placed in **cascade** with the plant. A lead compensator uses a zero at $-1/\tau$ and a pole at $-1/(\alpha\tau)$ with $\alpha < 1$, placing the zero *closer to the origin* and the pole *farther left* on the negative real axis.

## 4.4 Block-Level Compensator Type Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Uncompensated Open Loop<br>G of s H of s] --> Q{Design Goal?}
    Q -- Improve PM and transient response --> L1[Lead Compensator<br>Gc of s equals 1 plus tau s over 1 plus alpha tau s<br>alpha between 0 and 1]
    Q -- Improve steady state error --> L2[Lag Compensator<br>Gc of s equals 1 plus tau s over 1 plus beta tau s<br>beta greater than 1]
    Q -- Improve both --> L3[Lead Lag Compensator<br>cascade of lead and lag sections]
    L1 --> Out1[Net Effect:<br>Increased phase margin<br>Higher bandwidth<br>Faster response]
    L2 --> Out2[Net Effect:<br>Increased low frequency gain<br>Reduced ess<br>Minor phase effect]
    L3 --> Out3[Net Effect:<br>Better damping<br>and<br>Better accuracy]
```

## 4.5 Routh Array Layout (Schematic Template)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Row1["s to the n row"]
        A1[a0] --- A2[a2] --- A3[a4] --- A4[a6]
    end
    subgraph Row2["s to the n minus 1 row"]
        B1[a1] --- B2[a3] --- B3[a5] --- B4[a7]
    end
    subgraph Row3["s to the n minus 2 row"]
        C1[b1] --- C2[b2] --- C3[b3] --- C4[0]
    end
    subgraph Row4["s to the n minus 3 row"]
        D1[c1] --- D2[c2] --- D3[c3] --- D4[0]
    end
    Row1 --- Row2
    Row2 --- Row3
    Row3 --- Row4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each row's elements are calculated from the two rows above it. The leftmost column is the **first column** whose sign changes count the RHP roots.

---

## 4.6 Frequency-Domain Stability Margins (Nyquist/Bode Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Open Loop Frequency Response<br>G of j omega H of j omega] --> F2{At what frequency<br>is phase equals minus 180?}
    F2 -- Defines omega pc --> F3[At omega pc read magnitude<br>in dB]
    F3 -- Compute --> F4[GM in dB equals<br>minus 20 log of magnitude]
    F1 --> F5{At what frequency<br>is magnitude equals 0 dB?}
    F5 -- Defines omega gc --> F6[At omega gc read phase<br>in degrees]
    F6 -- Compute --> F7[PM equals 180 plus phase]
    F4 --> F8{Relative Stability<br>Assessment}
    F7 --> F8
    F8 -- Both positive --> Stable[Stable System<br>Higher GM and PM means more robust]
    F8 -- Negative --> Unstable[Unstable System<br>Reduce gain or add compensator]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What is the necessary condition for stability of a linear system?**

**Model Answer:**
The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a mathematical test applied to the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ of a linear time-invariant control system. It determines the number of closed-loop poles lying in the right-half s-plane by counting the sign changes in the first column of the Routh array. For absolute stability, the system characteristic equation must have **no roots in the right-half plane**, and therefore the first column of the Routh array must contain **no sign changes**.

**Necessary condition:** All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ must be **real, positive, and non-zero**. If any coefficient is zero, negative, or missing, the system *may* be unstable (or marginally stable), and the Routh array must be constructed to confirm.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a root locus? List any four rules for constructing the root locus on the s-plane.**

**Model Answer:**
A **root locus** is the graphical plot in the complex s-plane of the trajectories of the closed-loop poles of a feedback system as a system parameter (typically the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $+\infty$. Each point on the locus satisfies the angle condition $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ(2k+1)$.

**Four rules for construction:**
1. **Symmetry:** The root locus is symmetric about the real axis of the s-plane.
2. **Number of branches:** Equal to the number of open-loop poles $n$, starting at $K=0$ from each pole.
3. **Real-axis segments:** A real-axis point is on the locus if the total number of real poles and zeros to its right is **odd**.
4. **Asymptotes:** For $n - m$ branches going to infinity, the asymptote angles are $\theta_k = \dfrac{(2k+1)180^\circ}{n-m}$, intersecting the real axis at the centroid $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_k - \sum z_i}{n-m}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question Set B — Module 3 Internal Choice

---

### ❑ Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+3)(s+6)}$$

Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, determine:
- (i) the range of $K$ for which the system is stable, and
- (ii) the value of $K$ and the corresponding frequency $\omega$ at which the system becomes marginally stable.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the root locus of the system in part (a), clearly indicating:
- the asymptote angles and centroid,
- the breakaway point, and
- the $j\omega$-axis crossing.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Characteristic equation: $1 + G(s) = 0$

$$s(s+3)(s+6) + K = 0$$

Expand: $(s+3)(s+6) = s^2 + 9s + 18$, then multiply by $s$:

$$s^3 + 9s^2 + 18s + K = 0$$

**Step 2:** Routh array:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 18 \\
s^2 & 9 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{9 \cdot 18 - 1 \cdot K}{9} = \dfrac{162 - K}{9} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3:** For stability, first column must be all positive:

$$\frac{162 - K}{9} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 162 \qquad \text{and} \qquad K > 0$$

$$\boxed{0 < K < 162 \text{ for stability}}$$

**Step 4:** Marginal stability at $K = 162$. Auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row:

$$9s^2 + 162 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2 = -18 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j\sqrt{18} = \pm j\,3\sqrt{2} \approx \pm j\,4.243 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{K_{cr} = 162, \quad \omega_{cr} = 3\sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Poles:** $0, -3, -6$; **Zeros:** none. So $n = 3,\; m = 0$.

**Asymptotes:** $n - m = 3$ asymptotes with angles

$$\theta_0 = 60^\circ,\quad \theta_1 = 180^\circ,\quad \theta_2 = 300^\circ$$

**Centroid:**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{0 - 3 - 6}{3} = -3$$

**Real-axis locus:** Segments $[0, -3]$ and $(-\infty, -6]$ (odd pole count to the right).

**Breakaway point:** Solve $dK/ds = 0$ where $K = -(s^3 + 9s^2 + 18s)$:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 18s + 18) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2 + 6s + 6 = 0$$

$$s = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{36-24}}{2} = \frac{-6 \pm 2\sqrt{3}}{2} = -3 \pm \sqrt{3}$$

Numerically: $s_1 \approx -1.268$ (lies in $[0,-3]$ ✓) and $s_2 \approx -4.732$ (not in a locus segment ✗).

**Breakaway point:** $s \approx -1.268$

**$j\omega$ crossing:** From Routh analysis, at $K = 162$, $s = \pm j\,3\sqrt{2}$.

**Sketch description:** Branches from $0$ and $-3$ converge to breakaway at $-1.268$, then leave the real axis and curve symmetrically into the upper and lower half-planes, crossing the $j\omega$ axis at $\pm j\,3\sqrt{2}$ (when $K=162$), and finally extending to infinity along the $60^\circ, 180^\circ, 300^\circ$ asymptotes from the centroid at $-3$. The branch from $-6$ moves left along the real axis to $-\infty$ along the $180^\circ$ asymptote.

---

### ❑ Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s^2(s+5)}$$

Determine the angle of departure of the root locus from the complex pole at the origin (note: there are two coincident poles at $s=0$). Compute the centroid and asymptote angles. Identify any breakaway/break-in points.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the terms **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)**. For a system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{20}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$, determine the gain margin and phase margin. Comment on the relative stability of the system.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Poles:** Two at $s=0$ (repeated) and one at $s=-5$. **Zero:** $s=-2$. So $n=3,\; m=1$.

**Asymptotes:** $n - m = 2$ asymptotes.

Angles:

$$\theta_0 = \frac{180^\circ}{2} = 90^\circ,\quad \theta_1 = \frac{3 \cdot 180^\circ}{2} = 270^\circ = -90^\circ$$

**Centroid:**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0 + 0 - 5) - (-2)}{2} = \frac{-5 + 2}{2} = -1.5$$

**Angle of Departure from the pole at $s=0$:** For a test point $s_\varepsilon$ infinitesimally close to $s=0$ in the upper half-plane, the angle contributions are:

- From the *other* pole at $0$ (just below): $-90^\circ$
- From the pole at $-5$: angle from $0$ to $-5$ is $180^\circ$ (along negative real axis)
- From the zero at $-2$: angle from $0$ to $-2$ is $180^\circ$ (along negative real axis)

$$\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle(s_\varepsilon - z_i) - \sum \angle(s_\varepsilon - p_k) = 180^\circ + 180^\circ - (-90^\circ + 180^\circ) = 180^\circ + 180^\circ - 90^\circ = 270^\circ$$

But since this exceeds the principal range, we interpret it modulo $360^\circ$, so $\phi_d = 270^\circ = -90^\circ$.

**Breakaway/Break-in:** Compute $K$ from $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$s^2(s+5) + K(s+2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K = -\frac{s^2(s+5)}{s+2} = -\frac{s^3 + 5s^2}{s+2}$$

Differentiate $K$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero (using the quotient rule):

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -\frac{(3s^2 + 10s)(s+2) - (s^3+5s^2)(1)}{(s+2)^2} = 0$$

Numerator must vanish:

$$(3s^2 + 10s)(s+2) - (s^3 + 5s^2) = 0$$

Expand:

$$3s^3 + 6s^2 + 10s^2 + 20s - s^3 - 5s^2 = 2s^3 + 11s^2 + 20s = 0$$

$$s(2s^2 + 11s + 20) = 0$$

So $s = 0$ or $2s^2 + 11s + 20 = 0$:

$$s = \frac{-11 \pm \sqrt{121 - 160}}{4} = \frac{-11 \pm \sqrt{-39}}{4} = -2.75 \pm j\,1.561$$

**Break points:** $s = 0$ and $s \approx -2.75 \pm j\,1.561$.

**Real-axis locus:** Since there is a zero at $-2$, count to the right of trial points:
- For $s$ between $0$ and $-2$: 3 poles, 1 zero to the right (count: 3+1=4 even) → **NOT on locus** ✗
- Left of $-2$ but right of $-5$: 2 poles to the right → even → **NOT on locus** ✗
- Left of $-5$: 3 poles + 1 zero to the right = 4 → even → **NOT on locus** ✗

So **no real-axis segment** for this locus; the two branches from $s=0$ break away immediately into the complex plane and go to infinity along the $\pm 90^\circ$ asymptotes from centroid $-1.5$. The third branch from $s=-5$ moves toward the zero at $-2$ along the real axis.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Definitions:**
- **Gain Margin (GM):** The factor by which the open-loop gain may be increased at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180^\circ$) before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability. $GM = 1 / \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$. In dB: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- **Phase Margin (PM):** The additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be introduced at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = 1) before instability. $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.

**Computation for $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{20}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$:**

**Step 1 — Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{4}\right) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{4}\right) = 90^\circ$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1} A + \tan^{-1} B = 90^\circ$ iff $AB = 1$:

$$\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_{pc}}{4} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc}^2 = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{20}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2+4}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2+16}} = \frac{20}{2\sqrt{2}\sqrt{12}\sqrt{24}} = \frac{20}{2\sqrt{2}\cdot 2\sqrt{3}\cdot 2\sqrt{6}}$$

$$= \frac{20}{8\sqrt{36}} = \frac{20}{8 \cdot 6} = \frac{20}{48} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167$$

**Step 3 — Gain Margin:**

$$GM = \frac{1}{0.4167} = 2.4 \quad \text{(linear)} \qquad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(2.4) \approx 7.6 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$:** Set magnitude = 1:

$$\frac{20}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+4}\sqrt{\omega^2+16}} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega^2(\omega^2+4)(\omega^2+16) = 400$$

Let $x = \omega^2$: $x(x+4)(x+16) = 400 \;\Rightarrow\; x^3 + 20x^2 + 64x - 400 = 0$.

Solving numerically: $x \approx 3.07 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{gc} \approx 1.752$ rad/s.

**Step 5 — Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.752}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.752}{4}\right)$$

$$= 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.876) - \tan^{-1}(0.438) = 90^\circ - 41.22^\circ - 23.65^\circ = 25.13^\circ$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{GM \approx 7.6 \text{ dB}, \quad PM \approx 25^\circ}$$

**Comment on Relative Stability:** Both margins are positive, so the system is **stable**. However, the small phase margin (~25°) indicates that the closed-loop response will be **moderately oscillatory** with significant overshoot. The system is *marginally robust* — small parameter variations could push it close to instability. A compensator (lead or lead-lag) would be recommended to raise the PM to 45°–60° for a well-damped industrial-grade response.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Always write $G(s)H(s)$ in the **standard form** with $-s$ shifted signs: e.g., $(s+2)$, **not** $(2-s)$. Wrong sign in the characteristic equation → entire Routh array becomes wrong.
> 2. **First-column sign change counting:** Many students count sign changes in the *whole* array. KTU expects sign changes **only in the first column**.
> 3. **Forgetting the auxiliary equation:** A row of zeros does **not** mean "no solution." You must form the auxiliary equation and differentiate — otherwise the critical $K$ is *not* determined.
> 4. **Angle-of-departure direction:** The formula $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \Sigma\angle z - \Sigma\angle p$ requires angles measured *from* each pole/zero *to* the test point. Getting the direction wrong yields $\phi_d$ off by $180^\circ$.
> 5. **Breakaway validation:** Solving $dK/ds = 0$ gives candidates, but only those **on the real-axis locus segment** (Rule 3) are valid. Reject the others — a frequent KTU error.
> 6. **GM/PM in correct units:** GM in dB requires $20\log_{10}$ conversion; PM is already in degrees. Mixing them up is a common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### Routh-Hurwitz
- Characteristic equation must be written with all positive coefficients and in **descending powers of $s$**.
- **Necessary condition:** All coefficients present, real, same sign. **Not sufficient.**
- Routh array: each new row from two rows above; **first column sign changes = RHP roots**.
- **Zero in first column** (single zero) → replace with $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.
- **Entire row of zeros** → auxiliary equation $A(s)$ from row above, differentiate, replace.
- Marginal stability → auxiliary equation gives $s = \pm j\omega_{cr}$; substitute back for $K_{cr}$.

### Root Locus
- **Angle condition:** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ(2k+1)$ — defines locus.
- **Magnitude condition:** $K = 1/\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ — gives $K$ at any point.
- 3 poles, 0 zeros → asymptotes at $60^\circ, 180^\circ, 300^\circ$.
- 2 poles, 0 zeros → asymptotes at $\pm 90^\circ$.
- Real-axis segment rule: **odd** count of real poles+zeros to the right.
- Breakaway = solution of $dK/ds = 0$ on a valid real-axis segment.
- Angle of departure: $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle z - \sum \angle p$ from complex pole.
- Imaginary-axis crossing = Routh-Hurwitz critical point.

### Relative Stability
- **GM** measured at $\omega_{pc}$ (phase = $-180^\circ$): $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- **PM** measured at $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 0 dB): $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.
- For 2nd-order dominant: $\zeta \approx PM/100$ (in degrees).
- $PM > 0$ and $GM > 0$ (dB) → stable; higher values = more robust.

### Compensators
- **Lead:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1+\tau s}{1+\alpha\tau s}, \; 0 < \alpha < 1$ → adds positive phase, raises PM.
- **Lag:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1+\tau s}{1+\beta\tau s}, \; \beta > 1$ → boosts low-frequency gain, reduces $e_{ss}$.
- **Lead-Lag:** cascade of both → combined benefit.
- **Key trade-off:** Lead cannot improve steady-state error; Lag cannot improve transient/PM.
- **Design goal** in Module 3: identify the type, write the transfer function, sketch the Bode plot, and describe the qualitative effect on system performance.

### Quick Mental Shortcuts
- A Routh array with $K$ in the last row → boundary is when the row above goes to zero.
- If you see 3 poles on the negative real axis and one branch leaves toward $j\omega$ → breakaway on the **innermost** segment.
- If $PM < 30^\circ$ → "add a lead compensator." If $e_{ss}$ too large → "add a lag compensator." If both → "lead-lag."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Space Models - Introduction to the State Variable Concept, State Model of Mechanical and Electrical Systems. Various Forms of Representing State Space Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBTT413 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Space Models - Introduction to the State Variable Concept, State Model of Mechanical and Electrical Systems. Various Forms of Representing State Space Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STATE OF A SYSTEM (KTU Strict Definition):**
> The **state of a system** is the minimum set of variables (called *state variables*) such that the knowledge of these variables at time $t = t_0$, together with the knowledge of the input for time $t \ge t_0$, completely determines the behaviour of the system for all time $t \ge t_0$.

The **state variables** are physically meaningful quantities (positions, velocities, currents, voltages, etc.) whose present values summarize all the past history of the system. The **state space** is the *n-dimensional space* whose coordinate axes are formed by the state variables $x_1(t), x_2(t), \dots, x_n(t)$. The **state vector** is the column vector

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

and its trajectory in the state space is called the **state trajectory**.

### 1.2 Why Modern Control Theory Uses State Space?

Classical control theory uses the **transfer function** (input-output relation) and works only for **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)**, **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** systems. Modern control theory uses **state space** because:

- It handles **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)** systems naturally.
- It handles **time-varying** and **non-linear** systems.
- The internal dynamics (not just input-output) are fully exposed.
- It is directly suited for **digital computer** simulation.
- It enables optimal control, observer design, and Kalman filtering.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Car" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Your Car on Kerala Highway NH-66:**
> Imagine driving a car on a straight road. To fully describe *where the car will be 5 minutes from now*, you do **not** need to know its complete past history (every gear change, every horn honk). You only need:
> 1. Its current **position** $x_1$ (km marker)
> 2. Its current **velocity** $x_2$ (km/h)
> 3. The current **acceleration input** $u(t)$ (throttle/brake)
>
> The pair $(x_1, x_2)$ is the **state** of the car. Knowing these, plus the input $u(t)$ for the next 5 minutes, you can **predict** the future — exactly the role of state variables in a control system. If you add the steering angle as $x_3$ and yaw rate as $x_4$, the car becomes a 2D state model — and this is precisely how a self-driving car's controller works.

### 1.4 The General State-Space Equation

The state-space representation of an LTI system is a set of two coupled first-order matrix differential equations:

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) &= A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,u(t) \\
y(t) &= C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,u(t)
\end{aligned}}
$$

where the **system matrices** have the following KTU standard names:

| Symbol | KTU Name | Typical Dimension | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{x}(t)$ | State vector | $n \times 1$ | Energy-storing elements' quantities |
| $u(t)$ | Input (control) vector | $m \times 1$ | External forcing / actuator signal |
| $y(t)$ | Output vector | $p \times 1$ | Measured response |
| $A$ | System matrix | $n \times n$ | Internal dynamics / coupling |
| $B$ | Input matrix | $n \times m$ | How input enters the state |
| $C$ | Output matrix | $p \times n$ | How state is observed |
| $D$ | Direct feed-through matrix | $p \times m$ | Instantaneous input-output path |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Convention (2024 Scheme):**
> For SISO systems covered in the syllabus, $u(t)$ and $y(t)$ are scalars, so $B$ is an $n \times 1$ column, $C$ is a $1 \times n$ row, and $D$ is a $1 \times 1$ scalar. Always state the dimensions of $A, B, C, D$ when deriving a state model — examiners award **½ mark** per correctly dimensioned matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Choosing State Variables

For an *n-th order* system, we need **exactly *n* independent state variables**. The standard KTU selection rules are:

1. **Energy-storing elements** in a system are the natural choice:
   - **Mechanical (translational):** mass (M) → velocity, spring (K) → displacement
   - **Mechanical (rotational):** inertia (J) → angular velocity, torsion (K) → angular displacement
   - **Electrical:** inductor (L) → current, capacitor (C) → voltage
2. **Independent** — no state variable should be a linear combination of the others.
3. **Physical meaning preferred** — but mathematically any independent set works.
4. The total **order of the system** equals the number of independent energy storage elements.

### 2.2 State Model of Mechanical Translational Systems (Mass-Spring-Damper)

Consider the classic **M-K-B system** with mass $M$ (kg), spring constant $K$ (N/m), and viscous damping coefficient $B$ (N·s/m), with applied force $u(t)$ (N) and displacement $y(t)$ (m).

**Step 1 — Newton's second law:**

$$
M\,\ddot{y}(t) + B\,\dot{y}(t) + K\,y(t) = u(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Select state variables:**

$$
x_1(t) = y(t) \quad \text{(position)}, \qquad x_2(t) = \dot{y}(t) \quad \text{(velocity)}
$$

**Step 3 — Write first-order equations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \frac{1}{M}\left[u(t) - B\,x_2 - K\,x_1\right] = -\frac{K}{M}x_1 - \frac{B}{M}x_2 + \frac{1}{M}u
$$

**Step 4 — Output equation:**

$$
y(t) = x_1
$$

**Step 5 — Matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{K}{M} & -\dfrac{B}{M} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{M} \end{bmatrix} u(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0]\,u(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The system matrix $A$ has a characteristic polynomial $\det(sI - A) = s^2 + \frac{B}{M}s + \frac{K}{M}$, whose roots are the system's natural frequencies. This is the *mathematical fingerprint* of the system that remains invariant across all valid state-space representations.

### 2.3 State Model of Rotational Mechanical Systems

For a rotational mass-spring-damper with inertia $J$ (kg·m²), torsion $K$ (N·m/rad), damping $B$ (N·m·s/rad), torque $T(t)$:

$$
J\,\ddot{\theta}(t) + B\,\dot{\theta}(t) + K\,\theta(t) = T(t)
$$

This is **mathematically identical** to the translational form with the substitution $M \to J,\; y \to \theta,\; u \to T$. So the state model is

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta} \\ \ddot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -K/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \theta \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/J \end{bmatrix} T(t)
$$

### 2.4 State Model of Electrical Systems (Series RLC Circuit)

Consider a **series RLC** circuit with input voltage $v_i(t)$, output voltage across the capacitor $v_c(t) = y(t)$, resistance $R$, inductance $L$, capacitance $C$.

**Step 1 — KVL around the loop:**

$$
L\,\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i(t) + v_c(t) = v_i(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor relation:**

$$
i(t) = C\,\frac{dv_c}{dt} \implies \frac{di}{dt} = C\,\frac{d^2 v_c}{dt^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined second-order ODE:**

$$
LC\,\ddot{v}_c + RC\,\dot{v}_c + v_c = v_i(t)
$$

**Step 4 — State variables** (energy storage in L and C):

$$
x_1 = v_c \quad \text{(capacitor voltage)}, \qquad x_2 = i_L \quad \text{(inductor current)}
$$

**Step 5 — First-order equations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = \frac{1}{C}x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \frac{1}{L}\bigl[v_i - R\,x_2 - x_1\bigr] = -\frac{1}{L}x_1 - \frac{R}{L}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v_i
$$

**Step 6 — Matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \dfrac{1}{C} \\ -\dfrac{1}{L} & -\dfrac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} v_i
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

| # | System | Governing ODE | State Variables | System Matrix $A$ | Input Vector $B$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mass-Spring-Damper | $M\ddot{y}+B\dot{y}+Ky=u$ | $x_1=y,\;x_2=\dot{y}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -K/M & -B/M \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/M \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 2 | Rotational M-K-B | $J\ddot{\theta}+B\dot{\theta}+K\theta=T$ | $x_1=\theta,\;x_2=\dot{\theta}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -K/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/J \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 3 | Series RLC (output = $v_c$) | $LC\ddot{v}_c+RC\dot{v}_c+v_c=v_i$ | $x_1=v_c,\;x_2=i_L$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1/C \\ -1/L & -R/L \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/L \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 4 | Parallel RLC (output = $i_L$) | $LC\ddot{i}_L+RC\dot{i}_L+i_L = RC\,\dot{v}_i+i_{v_i}$ | $x_1=i_L,\;x_2=v_C$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1/L \\ -1/C & -1/RC \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/L \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 5 | Two-mesh circuit | Custom | Loop currents | Custom | Custom |

### 2.6 Various Forms of Representing State-Space Systems (KTU-Syllabus Mandatory)

For a general $n$-th order linear ODE

$$
\frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1}\frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \dot{y} + a_0 y = b_0 u
$$

the same system admits infinitely many state-space representations. The KTU syllabus requires mastery of the following **canonical / standard forms**:

#### (A) Phase-Variable Form (Controllable Canonical Form)

The state variables are the output and its successive derivatives:

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad \dots, \quad x_n = y^{(n-1)}
$$

The matrices take a special companion structure:

$$
A_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & \cdots & b_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = [0]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming the Companion Form:** The bottom row of $A_{PV}$ is always the negatives of the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial in *ascending* order. The $B$ vector has all zeros except a single 1 at the bottom — examiners instantly recognize this pattern.

#### (B) Diagonal Canonical Form (Decoupled / Modal Form)

When the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n$ of $A$ are distinct and real, the system can be put into the **diagonal** form via a similarity transformation $\mathbf{z} = P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$:

$$
A_D = P^{-1}AP = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B_D = P^{-1}B, \quad C_D = CP
$$

> **Why it matters:** In the diagonal form, the state equations are completely **decoupled** — each $z_i$ evolves independently. This is the *mathematical heart of modal analysis* and is used in fault diagnosis in aerospace structures (e.g., Boeing wing flutter analysis).

#### (C) Jordan Canonical Form (for repeated eigenvalues)

If $A$ has a repeated root $\lambda_i$ of multiplicity $m$, the diagonal form fails. Instead we get a **Jordan block** $J_i$ of size $m$:

$$
J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_i & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_i & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & \lambda_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### (D) Observable Canonical Form (Dual of Controllable Form)

$$
A_{O} = A_{PV}^{\,T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & -a_0 \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & -a_1 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & -a_2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
B_O = C_{PV}^{\,T} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 \\ b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_O = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check:** If the input matrix $B$ has a single 1 at the *bottom*, it is the **Controllable** / Phase-Variable form. If the output matrix $C$ has a single 1 at the *right*, it is the **Observable** form. This visual mnemonic saves 30 seconds in the exam hall.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft pitch dynamics is a 4-state system: $\alpha$ (angle of attack), $q$ (pitch rate), $\delta_e$ (elevator), $\theta$ (pitch angle). State-space enables full envelope flight control.
- **Robotics:** A 6-DOF robotic arm has 12 states (6 joint positions + 6 velocities). Classical TF methods are completely useless here.
- **Power Systems:** Multi-machine grid stability uses state-space models of 100+ order.
- **Automotive (ECU):** Engine control, ABS, and active suspension are all state observers (Kalman filters) running on the $A, B, C, D$ model in real-time.
- **Process Control:** MIMO distillation columns require state-space decoupling controllers — a hot research topic in KTU M.Tech theses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mass-Spring-Damper → State Space

**System:** A body of mass $M$ kg, attached to a wall via a spring of stiffness $K$ N/m and a damper of coefficient $B$ N·s/m, acted upon by an external force $u(t)$. The displacement of the body from equilibrium is $y(t)$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Force Balance**

Forces acting on the body (positive direction = right):
- External force: $+u(t)$
- Spring restoring force: $-K\,y(t)$ (opposes displacement)
- Damper viscous force: $-B\,\dot{y}(t)$ (opposes velocity)

Newton's second law $\sum F = M a$ gives:

$$
u(t) - K\,y(t) - B\,\dot{y}(t) = M\,\ddot{y}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Standard Form**

$$
M\,\ddot{y}(t) + B\,\dot{y}(t) + K\,y(t) = u(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Define State Variables**

Because the system is 2nd-order, we need 2 state variables. The two natural energy-related variables are the displacement and the velocity of the mass:

$$
x_1(t) \triangleq y(t), \qquad x_2(t) \triangleq \dot{y}(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Derivatives**

$$
\dot{x}_1(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[y(t)] = \dot{y}(t) = x_2(t)
$$

For $\dot{x}_2$, divide the ODE by $M$ and isolate $\ddot{y}$:

$$
\ddot{y}(t) = \frac{1}{M}u(t) - \frac{B}{M}\dot{y}(t) - \frac{K}{M}y(t)
$$

Therefore,

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = -\frac{K}{M}x_1(t) - \frac{B}{M}x_2(t) + \frac{1}{M}u(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Write as State Equation (vector-matrix form)**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{K}{M} & -\dfrac{B}{M} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{M} \end{bmatrix} u(t)
$$

**Step 6 — Output Equation**

If we measure the displacement, the output is $y(t) = x_1(t)$:

$$
y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0]\,u(t)
$$

**Step 7 — Verify the Characteristic Polynomial**

Compute $\det(sI - A)$:

$$
\det\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ K/M & s + B/M \end{bmatrix} \right) = s\!\left(s + \frac{B}{M}\right) - (-1)\!\left(\frac{K}{M}\right)
$$

$$
= s^2 + \frac{B}{M}s + \frac{K}{M}
$$

Multiplying by $M$ we get $Ms^2 + Bs + K$, which exactly matches the characteristic polynomial of the ODE. **Self-consistency check passed.** ✔

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Series RLC Circuit → State Space

**System:** Series connection of $R$, $L$, $C$ driven by voltage source $v_i(t)$. Output is the capacitor voltage $v_c(t)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Loop Current**

Let the loop current be $i(t)$ flowing clockwise.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)**

$$
v_i(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)
$$

Substituting element laws $v_R = iR$, $v_L = L\,\frac{di}{dt}$, $v_C = v_c$:

$$
v_i(t) = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di}{dt} + v_c(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor i-v Relation**

$$
i(t) = C\,\frac{dv_c}{dt} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{di}{dt} = C\,\frac{d^2 v_c}{dt^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate the Current to Get an ODE in $v_c$**

Differentiate the KVL equation with respect to time, or substitute the capacitor relation directly. A cleaner path: solve the KVL for $\frac{di}{dt}$:

$$
L\,\frac{di}{dt} = v_i - R\,i - v_c
$$

But $i = C\,\dot{v}_c$, so $\frac{di}{dt} = C\,\ddot{v}_c$. Substituting:

$$
L\,C\,\ddot{v}_c = v_i - R\,C\,\dot{v}_c - v_c
$$

Rearranging:

$$
LC\,\ddot{v}_c + RC\,\dot{v}_c + v_c = v_i
$$

**Step 5 — Choose State Variables (L and C store energy)**

$$
x_1 \triangleq v_c \quad \text{(electric energy in capacitor: } \tfrac{1}{2}Cv_c^2\text{)}, \quad x_2 \triangleq i_L \quad \text{(magnetic energy in inductor: } \tfrac{1}{2}Li_L^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 6 — Express First Derivatives**

From $i_L = C\,\dot{v}_c = C\,\dot{x}_1$:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = \frac{1}{C}\,x_2
$$

From the KVL $L\dot{x}_2 = v_i - R x_2 - x_1$:

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{1}{L}x_1 - \frac{R}{L}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v_i
$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the State Equation**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \dfrac{1}{C} \\ -\dfrac{1}{L} & -\dfrac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} v_i
$$

**Step 8 — Output Equation**

$$
y = v_c = x_1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify the Characteristic Polynomial**

$$
\det(sI - A) = \det\!\begin{bmatrix} s & -1/C \\ 1/L & s + R/L \end{bmatrix} = s\!\left(s+\frac{R}{L}\right) - \left(-\frac{1}{C}\right)\!\left(\frac{1}{L}\right)
$$

$$
= s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC}
$$

Multiplying by $LC$ gives $LCs^2 + RCs + 1$, which exactly matches the characteristic polynomial of the governing ODE. **Verification passed.** ✔

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Phase-Variable Form from ODE

**Given ODE:**

$$
\dddot{y} + 6\ddot{y} + 11\dot{y} + 6\,y = u(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Identify Order and Coefficients**

The system is 3rd-order, so $n=3$. The characteristic polynomial is

$$
s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = 0
$$

Coefficients: $a_2 = 6,\; a_1 = 11,\; a_0 = 6$.

**Step 2 — Define Phase Variables**

$$
x_1 = y, \qquad x_2 = \dot{y}, \qquad x_3 = \ddot{y}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the First Three Derivatives Trivially**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = x_3
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE for the Highest Derivative**

$$
\dddot{y} = -6y - 11\dot{y} - 6\ddot{y} + u = -6x_1 - 11x_2 - 6x_3 + u
$$

So,

$$
\dot{x}_3 = -6x_1 - 11x_2 - 6x_3 + u
$$

**Step 5 — Write in Matrix Form**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Pattern Recognition (KTU Trick)**

For the ODE $\dddot{y} + a_2\ddot{y} + a_1\dot{y} + a_0 y = u$, the last row of $A$ is $\begin{bmatrix} -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$. The $B$ vector is the canonical unit vector $e_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$. Always check this pattern before writing the matrices in the answer script — it is the fastest way to earn the full 7 marks on a 14-mark problem.

### 3.4 Conversion Between Representations (Similarity Transformation)

Given a state-space model $(A, B, C, D)$, a new representation $(\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}, \tilde{C}, \tilde{D})$ obtained via a non-singular transformation matrix $P$ such that $\mathbf{x} = P\mathbf{z}$ is

$$
\tilde{A} = P^{-1}AP, \quad \tilde{B} = P^{-1}B, \quad \tilde{C} = CP, \quad \tilde{D} = D
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Invariance Theorem:** The characteristic polynomial $\det(sI - A)$, the eigenvalues, the transfer function $C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$, controllability, and observability are all **invariant** under similarity transformation. Only the state variables change their *appearance*, not the underlying physical system.

### 3.5 Transfer Function From State Space (Reverse Map)

Starting from the state equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + Bu$, $y = C\mathbf{x} + Du$, taking the Laplace transform assuming zero initial conditions:

$$
sX(s) = AX(s) + BU(s) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}B\,U(s)
$$

$$
Y(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B\,U(s) + D\,U(s)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D}
$$

This formula is essential for KTU problems that ask: *"From the state model, find the transfer function."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The General State-Space Model (Mermaid)

The following functional architecture illustrates the flow of signals in a state-space model. Note: input, state, and output signals are shown with their physical interpretations, mimicking the canonical block diagram taught in KTU lectures.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[u(t)<br/>INPUT] --> sumAdd((+))
    sumAdd --> matB[B Matrix<br/>n x m]
    matB --> intNode1[Integrator<br/>1 over s]
    intNode1 --> stateVec[x(t)<br/>State Vector]
    stateVec --> matA[A Matrix<br/>n x n]
    matA --> sumAdd
    stateVec --> matC[C Matrix<br/>p x n]
    matC --> sumAdd2((+))
    inputNode --> matD[D Matrix<br/>p x m]
    matD --> sumAdd2
    sumAdd2 --> outNode[y(t)<br/>OUTPUT]
    stateVec --> fb1[Feedback Path]
    fb1 --> matA
```

> **Read this diagram as:** The input $u(t)$ passes through $D$ to the output (direct path) and through $B$ to drive the integrator, producing the next state $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$. The current state is fed back through $A$ to be summed with $B u$, closing the loop. The same state is sensed by $C$ to produce the output $y(t)$.

### 4.2 Physical Schematic — Mass-Spring-Damper (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    forceNode[Applied Force u t] --> sum1((+))
    springNode[Spring K x1] --> sum1
    damperNode[Damper B x2] --> sum1
    sum1 --> massNode[Mass M]
    massNode --> x2Node[Velocity x2]
    x2Node --> posInt[Integrator over s]
    posInt --> x1Node[Position x1]
    x1Node --> springNode
    x1Node --> outNode[Output y t]
    x2Node --> damperNode
```

### 4.3 Physical Schematic — Series RLC Circuit (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcNode[Voltage Source v sub i] --> sumKVL((KVL Sum))
    sumKVL --> resNode[Resistor R x2]
    sumKVL --> indNode[Inductor L]
    sumKVL --> capNode[Capacitor C]
    indNode --> curNode[Current x2]
    curNode --> capInt[Integrator 1 over sC]
    capInt --> vCapNode[Voltage x1]
    vCapNode --> capNode
    vCapNode --> outNode[Output y t]
    curNode --> resNode
```

### 4.4 Signal-Flow Graph of Phase-Variable Form

For the generic 3rd-order system $\dddot{y} + a_2\ddot{y} + a_1\dot{y} + a_0 y = u$, the signal-flow graph is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inU[u] --> sumS((+))
    sumS --> gainN[Gain 1]
    gainN --> nodeX3[x3]
    nodeX3 --> gainY2[Gain 1 over s]
    gainY2 --> nodeX2[x2]
    nodeX2 --> gainY1[Gain 1 over s]
    gainY1 --> nodeX1[x1]
    nodeX1 --> gainY3[Gain 1 over s]
    gainY3 --> nodeY[y]
    nodeY --> gainA0[Gain a0]
    nodeX2 --> gainA1[Gain a1]
    nodeX3 --> gainA2[Gain a2]
    gainA0 --> negSum((-))
    gainA1 --> negSum
    gainA2 --> negSum
    negSum --> sumS
    nodeX1 --> outY[Output y]
```

> **Interpretation:** The signal flows rightward from $u$ to $y$ through three cascaded integrators (1/s blocks), and the bottom row of feedback gains $-a_0, -a_1, -a_2$ closes the loop, implementing the characteristic polynomial in pure signal-flow form. This is *Mason's gain formula* in its most aesthetic form.

### 4.5 Conversion Flow Chart: ODE ↔ State-Space ↔ Transfer Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    odeBox[ODE Form] -- Phase Variable --> sspvBox[State Space PVF]
    odeBox -- Controllable Canonical --> sscBox[State Space CCF]
    odeBox -- Observable Canonical --> ssoBox[State Space OCF]
    sspvBox -- Similarity Transform --> sscBox
    sscBox -- Similarity Transform --> ssoBox
    sscBox -- Laplace and C sI-A inv B --> tfBox[Transfer Function G s]
    tfBox -- Partial Fractions --> sspvBox
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
